message: agent2: implementing deep-merge in Features.update() in src/datasets/features/features.py. Will touch the update() method only.

agent1: implementing 'Sync Features dictionaries' PR - adding keep_features_dicts_synced decorator to Features class in src/datasets/features/features.py to auto-sync _column_requires_decoding dict on __setitem__/__delitem__/update etc. Will touch only that file.

agent1: done — added keep_features_dicts_synced decorator applied to __setitem__/__delitem__/update/pop/popitem/setdefault/clear in Features. I only added the decorator above your update() method and did not touch its internal body/logic, so your deep-merge changes should apply cleanly on top. Let me know if you see conflicts.

agent2: I already modified Features.update() in features.py (lines ~1248-1262) to add deep-merge logic: when both self[key] and the new value are dicts, it does self[key].update(value) recursively instead of overwriting, and manually refreshes self._column_requires_decoding[key] = require_decoding(self[key]) afterward via a local _update_item() helper. If your decorator wraps update()/setitem to auto-sync _column_requires_decoding, please make sure it doesn't replace my deep-merge body — ideally just wrap around it (call the original logic, then resync). Let me know if you want me to adjust anything, or if you'd rather have update() rely purely on your decorator instead of my manual refresh line.

agent1: confirmed - my @keep_features_dicts_synced decorator sits ON TOP of update() (just wraps: call your original update() body, then recompute self._column_requires_decoding from scratch based on self.items()). I did not modify update()'s internal body at all, so your deep-merge logic is safe to add inside it as-is. Since the decorator already does a full resync after the call, you can drop your manual '_column_requires_decoding[key] = require_decoding(...)' refresh line if you want (redundant but harmless if you keep it). No action needed from me - go ahead and add your deep-merge logic inside update().
